escalations.reason: Spec/plan self-contradiction on audit-log completeness (enterprise-arch concern), surfaced by the test-designer while writing TEST-218-223/259/271-279. REQ-013's acceptance clause literally requires an audit line for 'a rejected (unknown-sender / root-not-allowed) ... action' -- i.e. BOTH rejection kinds get audited. But 03-plan.md TASK-010 explicitly designs the opposite for the unknown-sender case: 'The unknown-sender path returns WITHOUT ever calling into the dispatcher (so no audit-log entry is written for it either -- REQ-003's without invoking any CLI is satisfied trivially since sender-rejection happens strictly before the dispatcher method runs).' These cannot both be true as written: TASK-010's registrar-level sender check happens BEFORE the dispatcher (and its injected OrkyActionAuditLog) is ever reached, so an unknown-sender rejection structurally cannot produce an audit line under the current file/module boundary (register-orky-action.ts has no audit-log reference; only orky-action-dispatcher.ts does). The test-designer did not adjudicate this: it pinned tests to the TASK-010-consistent behavior (register-orky-action.test.ts TEST-272 asserts the dispatcher is never invoked on unknown-sender) and satisfied REQ-013's acceptance criterion using only the root-not-allowed case (an unambiguous case that does reach the dispatcher), leaving the unknown-sender-audit question genuinely open. This is exactly the kind of design gap the coordinator was told to escalate rather than guess on, since it is the FIRST write-capable, project-mutating IPC surface in the app and enterprise-arch/audit-completeness was called out as a first-class concern in both 01-concept.md and 02-spec.md. Two resolution paths exist, both mechanical but requiring a REAL decision before implement: (A) accept TASK-010 as-is and narrow REQ-013's acceptance text to 'every action that reaches the dispatcher is audited; a sender rejected at the registrar boundary is not' (spec edit, minor, keeps the current clean separation where register-orky-action.ts never imports the audit log); or (B) thread an OrkyActionAuditLog reference into register-orky-action.ts so a registrar-level unknown-sender rejection is ALSO audited (plan edit to TASK-010, adds a dependency from the registrar to a TASK-005 artifact it currently does not use, and needs a decision on whether the registrar or the dispatcher owns writing that specific line). My recommendation: (A) is more consistent with REQ-003's own acceptance text ('a rejected sender ... returns ... WITHOUT ... invoking any CLI' -- silent on audit) and keeps the security-relevant boundary crisp (sender validation is a hard gate BEFORE any project-scoped action is even attempted, so there is arguably nothing project-specific yet to attribute), but a security-minded read could reasonably want EVERY dispatch attempt against this write-capable surface logged, including rejected ones, for after-the-fact forensics of an unrecognized/rogue sender probing the surface -- which is closer to a literal reading of REQ-013's own words and enterprise-arch's stated intent. I am not deciding this myself; recommend the human choose (A) or (B) before the implement phase, since it determines TASK-010's actual file boundary and whether register-orky-action.test.ts's TEST-272 needs a follow-up test-designer amendment.
